posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
I only became aware of this producer this year through various posts but mostly William Kelley’s. I was very happy with both the 2018 white and red Rully (I posted about this here). Well it seems that I’ll now have to be lucky to try some of their cuvées!

As mentioned a few times on this forum by people who buy wine in Quebec, highly allocated bottles are sold through our provincial monopoly through a lottery system (win the right to buy). This has been true for a while for most of the standard culprits (DRC, Rousseau, Leroy, Raveneau, Rayas, Clos Rougeard, etc.).

Well, well, our last lottery now has Dureuil Janthial Rully Premier Cru Grésigny 2018 (54 bottles, max qty of 1 per person), Margotés 2018 (572 bottles, max qty of 2 per person) and even En Guesnes 2018 (241 bottles, max qty of 1 per person).

My question to you is: Is this a trend? Is the secret out on Dureuil-Janthial?

If it’s still easy to source everywhere else, I’ll retain hope that it’s only our monopoly needing to fill the list of products for the latest 2021 lottery and that’s the only thing they had on hand.
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: If you don’t want the secret to be out don’t post about it. That being said it’s readily available in the US at low prices.

posts.name: John Webber
posts.cooked: The largest holding on CT of any of their listed wines is 166 bottles. With those kind of #'s, I won’t bother looking for it on the shelf deep in the heart of TX any time soon.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: one of the best bourgogne blanc i have had in recent memory was the 18 from dureuil-janthial. exceptional, and under $30 to boot. it was decidedly better than some of his rully village i have had from other vintages. that being said, they don’t seem too difficult to acquire.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The secret is indeed out! You used to be able to find multiple vintages in stock in the US and UK markets at least; now, each vintage sells through before the next arrives. Prices are creeping up as well, though they’re still very modest by contemporary Côte d’Or standards. Vincent has been making great wines for almost thirty years, and I think it’s amply deserved. And I hope it’s symptomatic of a wine culture that’s becoming a bit less fixated on appellation. Anyone who tasted Dureuil’s wines looking for value in the context of rising prices in the Côte de Beaune will have found that his wines are also better than a lot of wines being made in the Côte de Beaune, too.

Anyone interested in Dureuil’s methods might enjoy my article of earlier this year (sorry, it’s behind the paywall!) Robert Parker Wine Advocate

And if you like Dureuil’s wines, check out his protégé Maxime Cottenceau.

While we’re on the subject of the Côte Chalonnaise, Gautier Desvignes in Givry is showing a lot of promise, too, since he took over the family domaine a couple of years ago.

posts.name: Andreas Bram
posts.cooked:
Yes indeed a great producer. The Bourgogne Blanc is just amazing for its appellation.
I actually also really like his reds, even though he is more known for the whites.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: A couple weeks ago I found a 2001 Dureuil-Janthial Nuits St. Georges 1er Cru Clos des Argillières Cuvée Unique Vieilles Vignes (I think they accidentally left out “Supercalifragilisticexpialidocious”) and look forward to giving it a taste.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: I still have a bottle or two each of the 91 and 92 Argillieres from Monique Naudin, Vincent’s mother in law, from whom this parcel comes. Refulgent and sweaty, about as far from modern burgundy as can be imagined but very stimulating indeed.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: Has become so much harder to find.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked: So a new run will start and one of the last fairly priced top Burgundy will be 3 figures soon. Fine.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
The secret is indeed out! You used to be able to find multiple vintages in stock in the US and UK markets at least; now, each vintage sells through before the next arrives. Prices are creeping up as well, though they’re still very modest by contemporary Côte d’Or standards. Vincent has been making great wines for almost thirty years, and I think it’s amply deserved. And I hope it’s symptomatic of a wine culture that’s becoming a bit less fixated on appellation. Anyone who tasted Dureuil’s wines looking for value in the context of rising prices in the Côte de Beaune will have found that his wines are also better than a lot of wines being made in the Côte de Beaune, too.

Anyone interested in Dureuil’s methods might enjoy my article of earlier this year (sorry, it’s behind the paywall!) Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

And if you like Dureuil’s wines, check out his protégé Maxime Cottenceau.

While we’re on the subject of the Côte Chalonnaise, Gautier Desvignes in Givry is showing a lot of promise, too, since he took over the family domaine a couple of years ago.

William, with regard to recent vintages (say, 2017, 2018, and 2019) are there major differences in the quality/style of the vintages between the Cote d’Or and the Cote Chalonnaise for either reds or whites? Thanks.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: I blame William Kelley - he seems unable to keep a secret!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William, with regard to recent vintages (say, 2017, 2018, and 2019) are there major differences in the quality/style of the vintages between the Cote d’Or and the Cote Chalonnaise for either reds or whites? Thanks.

There are definitely differences in style between the vintages. I’ll confine my remarks to Dureuil because generalizations are difficult. The 2017s are the highest in acidity and the most classically balanced: tightly wound whites with great acid structure; supple, charming reds. The 2018s are amazingly successful for the vintage, with uncommon mid-palate depth in the whites (this vintage is often a bit dilute as a whole) and no excess of maturity in either color. The 2019s are the most concentrated and also have great acid in white: I suspect they’ll shut down hard, given how much substance there is in the bottle. The 2019 reds you might think of as a somewhat richer, more textural version of the very charming, pretty 2017s.

To answer what, on re-reading, I see you were actually asking, there are indeed some minor differences—a bit more rain in the Chalonnaise in summer 2018, for example, which was helpful—but the big picture is much the same across the region.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
So a new run will start and one of the last fairly priced top Burgundy will be 3 figures soon. Fine.

I do take your point, and that’s the downside of wine criticism for informed consumers as we have discussed before. But equally, how much did you buy when they were very cheap and widely available? We all had 26 years and counting to stock up, and one could buy 2014s, 2015s direct from the importer two years ago. If you have a big stash, your foresight should soften the blow; if you didn’t, can you really complain?

The fact that the market is waking up to how good these wines are sends a great message to the next generation in the Côte Chalonnaise, and can only be a good thing for the region. Vincent Dureuil has 17 hectares: Rully has 357 hectares in village and premier cru AOCs… so there is a lot of potential for anyone who wants to do the work.

posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
Opened 2014 Dureuil-Janthial Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Champs-Gain last weekend and it was mind blowing. The wine of an absolute grace! I am curious how some of the Grand Crus would sing in Vincent’s cellars if he ever gets his hands on such grapes. Some vineyards are blessed with top winemakers working with them. I wish Vincent had the access too.

And I adore all the range of his wines and happy to drink Bourgogne Blanc, but unfortunately it very quickly disappears from my cellar.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Opened 2014 Dureuil-Janthial Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Champs-Gain last weekend and it was mind blowing. The wine of an absolute grace! I am curious how some of the Grand Crus would sing in Vincent’s cellars if he ever gets his hands on such grapes. Some vineyards are blessed with top winemakers working with them. I wish Vincent had the access too.

It’s tempting to pose this question, but in so doing, we are actually validating the hierarchy that has been holding places such as Rully back! If the Meix Cadot Vieilles Vinges is better than most Bâtard-Montrachet, why assume that Vincent’s Bâtard-Montrachet, if he were to make one, would be better than his Meix Cadot Vieilles Vinges? Once one starts putting the question that way, instead, things get really interesting.

posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:

Once one starts putting the question that way, instead, things get really interesting.

I fully agree with you, William. That just confirms my notion, that some vineyards are blessed with top winemakers working with them. When I look at Champagne, I right away think of Olivier Collin and Cedric Bouchard. They turn potato fields into grand crus )))

posts.name: Peter Hirsch
posts.cooked: I happened to be staying in the town of Rully, summer of 2013. I wandered over to D-J w/o an appointment, they couldn’t have been nicer, great tour, nice tasting, all great. So I started to buy their wines because of the personal attachment. My favorite (like others) was the Meix Cadot VV. I could rarely find in the US, but was easy to find in London and ship home. Not overly expensive for the quality, I could find it by the case quantity. That became harder by about 2017 or so, so I switched to buying the ‘straight’ Meix Cadot which was findable in the USA w/o huge problems. And now. Can’t find either, either here or London. A quick check of CT shows not a bottle offered of either the MC or the MCVV either here or in London, any vintage. Oh well…

Yes : indeed things, in burgundy, are getting interesting. A place of made plus a place of born. Producers, producers, and …

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I happened to be staying in the town of Rully, summer of 2013. I wandered over to D-J w/o an appointment, they couldn’t have been nicer, great tour, nice tasting, all great. So I started to buy their wines because of the personal attachment. My favorite (like others) was the Meix Cadot VV. I could rarely find in the US, but was easy to find in London and ship home. Not overly expensive for the quality, I could find it by the case quantity. That became harder by about 2017 or so, so I switched to buying the ‘straight’ Meix Cadot which was findable in the USA w/o huge problems. And now. Can’t find either, either here or London. A quick check of CT shows not a bottle offered of either the MC or the MCVV either here or in London, any vintage. Oh well…

Should note that Vincent has halted all sales for a year in the aftermath of the 2021 frosts, which completely ravaged his vineyards. So that will not help supply.

posts.cooked: Since we are already talking about these wines, I see that my local wine shop has his Rully Blanc 2018 at $48. Will this wine offer a taste of what the excitement is about or does one need to move up the classification scale? Thanks.

posts.name: C. Keller
posts.cooked: Big fan of the Meix Cadot VV. Its very limited, disappears quickly and I always pounce at the first offer I see. Its one of the few WS alerts I have set and out of paranoia I always check it every week. Haven’t seen any 2019 yet this year which seems late but, again could be paranoia.

It should!

For what it’s worth, here is my first taste of the Rully 2018: https://www.wineberserkers.com/forum/viewtopic.php?p=3241029
I liked it and plan on liking it over the next few years.

Nice notes!

Vincent’s vines in the communal AOC are nicely located, which helps. By contrast, a lot of generic Rully comes from the plateau above the village, which is not really on the same level as the more historic vineyards around the village and in the combe.
